full_text:
STATE OF LOUISIANA
 COURT OF APPEAL, THIRD CIRCUIT

 15-153

JAMES E. STEWART, SR.

VERSUS

DIANNE DENLEY STEWART

 **********

 APPEAL FROM THE
 FIRST JUDICIAL DISTRICT COURT
 PARISH OF CADDO, NO. 441,913
 HONORABLE. JOSEPH BLEICH, DISTRICT JUDGE

 **********

 JOHN E. CONERY
 JUDGE

 **********

Court composed of Jimmie C. Peters, John E. Conery, and David Kent Savoie,
Judges.

 AFFIRMED.

Donald L. Kneipp
Post Office Drawer 2808
Monroe, Louisiana 71207
(318) 388-4440
COUNSEL FOR DEFENDANT/APPELLANT:
 Dianne Denley Stewart
 James C. McMichael, Jr.
McMichael, Medlin, D'Anna, Wedgeworth & Lafargue, LLC
Post Office Box 72
Shreveport, Louisiana 71161-0072
(318) 221-1004
COUNSEL FOR PLAINTIFF/APPELLEE:
 James E. Stewart, Sr.

Nyle A. Politz
Booth, Lockard, Politz & Lesage
920 Pierremont Road, Suite 104
Shreveport, Louisiana 71106
(318) 222-2333
COUNSEL FOR PLAINTIFF/APPELLEE:
 James E. Stewart, Sr.
 CONERY, Judge.

 Dianne Denley Stewart (Ms. Denley) appeals the trial court's judgment of

September 30, 2014, which granted the peremptory exception of no cause of action

asserted by her former husband James E. Stewart, Sr. (Mr. Stewart).1 The trial

court found that the relief sought by Ms. Denley in her petition was a substantive

amendment of the April 27, 2010 final judgment of partition, which is precluded

under Louisiana law. For the following reasons, we affirm.

 FACTS AND PROCEDURAL HISTORY

 The parties to this litigation were married in 1981, divorced on July 18, 2000,

and the community property regime was terminated retroactively to July 13, 1999.

After a trial on the issue of division of community property and the submission of

post-trial memorandum, oral reasons were given by the trial court on November 16,

2005, and a judgment of partition of community property was signed on December

2, 2005. That judgment ordered that the retirement interest of Mr. Stewart in the

Louisiana State Employees' Retirement System (LASERS), and the retirement

interest of Ms. Denley in the Teachers Retirement System of Louisiana (TRSL),

would be divided according to the formula devised in Sims v. Sims, 358 So.2d 919

(La.1978). The trial court further ordered counsel "to prepare the appropriate order

for the court's approval without delay."

 However, by February 14, 2008, the issue of retirement benefits was not yet

entirely resolved, and the trial court once again ordered the division of the

retirement interests of both parties according to the Sims formula, except for the

 1
 James E. Stewart, Sr. is a member of the Louisiana Second Circuit Court of Appeal. All
members of the Second Circuit Court of Appeal signed an order of recusal on January 20, 2015.
The Louisiana Supreme Court issued an order on January 27, 2015 assigning the case to the
Court of Appeal, Third Circuit. For the purposes of this opinion we will refer to him as Mr.
Stewart.
 entitlement of Ms. Denley to survivor benefits. The issue of LASERS's survivor

benefits was then pending before the supreme court in the case of LASERS v.

McWilliams, 06-2191, (La. 12/2/08), 996 So.2d 1036. At the time of the trial

court's February 14, 2008 ruling, LASERS v. McWilliams was not a final ruling, as

a request for rehearing had been filed in that case.

 The trial court ordered the parties to submit a final prepared judgment within

thirty days of a final ruling in LASERS v. McWilliams:

 [P]roposed orders dividing the LASERS retirement interest of JAMES
 E. STEWART, SR. and the Louisiana Teachers Retirement interest of
 DIANNE DENLEY. The Court will, without necessity of further
 proceeding herein [argument or hearing], execute and enter the
 appropriate order in conformity with the Judgment of Partition
 previously rendered herein and the Lasers vs. McWilliams decision.

 Finally, on April 15, 2010, a "Second Amended Judgment of Partition of

Employment Benefits" was signed by the trial court granting Mr. Stewart his

community interest in Ms. Denley's TRSL retirement account. On April 27, 2010,

a "Corrected Judgment of Partition of Employment Benefits" (April 27, 2010

Judgment) was signed by the trial court granting Ms. Denley her community

interest in Mr. Stewart's LASERS retirement account. The April 27, 2010

Judgment also clarified Ms. Denley's rights to survivor benefits in accordance with

the supreme court's decision in LASERS v. McWilliams. The April 27, 2010

Judgment was approved as to form by counsel for Ms. Denley and was requested

in order to correct the handwritten portion of the identical judgment signed by the

trial court on March 15, 2010.

 The April 27, 2010 Judgment also ordered Ms. Denley to provide LASERS

with a certified copy of the judgment within thirty days of signing. Likewise, in

 2
 the April 15, 2010 judgment partitioning Ms. Denley's benefits in the TRSL, Mr.

Stewart was also ordered to provide a certified copy of the judgment to the TRSL.

 On July 31, 2014, Ms. Denley, pro se, filed a petition entitled, "Rule to

Show Cause to Amend Judgment of Partition," which was fixed for hearing on

October 2, 2014. In her petition, Ms. Denley sought to amend the April 27, 2010

Judgment establishing her community property interest in Mr. Stewart's LASERS

account made "payable to Dianne Denley upon retirement/termination of

employment of James E. Stewart, Sr." Ms. Denley sought to incorporate language

which would "allow her to immediately [begin] drawing her share of his LASER'S

pension; or that James Stewart, Sr. pay to mover directly 40% of his current salary

and/or the amount of mover's share of the pension."

 Ms. Denley's basis for seeking the amendment apparently arises from her

retirement from teaching in 2014, which triggered the payment to Mr. Stewart of

twenty percent of her retirement benefits ordered in the trial court's April 16, 2010

judgment. Citing the inequities of the situation, as Mr. Stewart has not chosen to

retire, Ms. Denley stated in her petition that the April 27, 2010 Judgment was not a

final judgment pursuant to La.R.S. 9:2801(B). In response, Mr. Stewart filed the

declinatory exception of lack of subject matter jurisdiction and the peremptory

exceptions of no cause of action and res judicata.

 Although the hearing on all issues was originally fixed for October 2, 2014,

the trial judge, sitting ad hoc, moved the hearing to September 23, 2014 in order to

rule before his appointment expired on September 30, 2014. On the day before the

hearing, Ms. Denley sought a continuance due to the inability of conditionally

retained counsel to appear, and/or to have more time for her to retain counsel.

Counsel for Mr. Stewart had not received a copy of the motion to continue, and did

 3
 not have the authority to consent to the requested continuance. At the September

23, 2014 hearing, the trial court expressed its intention to move forward due to the

need to expedite the matter considering the parties involved, and the possibility

that additional recusals could ensue.

 The trial court did allow counsel for Ms. Denley, or Ms. Denley pro se, to

submit any opposition to the motions on or before noon on September 29, 2014.

Counsel for Mr. Stewart waived oral argument and agreed to submit the issue to

the trial court on his previously filed brief. The continuance sought by Ms. Denley

was denied by the trial court at the hearing.

 In an order dated September 24, 2014, the request for continuance by Ms.

Denley was denied. The order also memorialized the procedure for submission of

the motions for decision on briefs. Ms. Denley's retained counsel timely submitted

a brief in response to the trial court's order on September 29, 2014. As Mr.

Stewart had previously submitted his brief on the issue, the matter was properly

submitted to the trial court for decision.

 On September 30, 2014, the trial court issued its judgment, granting Mr.

Stewart's peremptory exception of no cause of action and rendering moot the

exceptions of res judicata and lack of subject matter jurisdiction filed by Mr.

Stewart. Ms. Denley timely appealed on October 21, 2014. Shortly thereafter, Mr.

Stewart sought to supplement the record with letters from both LASERS and

TRSL, which Ms. Denley opposed. Despite the inclusion of the letters in the

record on appeal, there was no hearing or order granting Mr. Stewart's motion to

supplement the record. Ms. Denley filed a "Motion and Designation of Contents

of Record," which was denied by the trial court as untimely.

 4
 ASSIGNMENTS OF ERROR

 Assignment of Error No. 1: The Trial Court's Granting of the
 Exception of No Cause of Action Was Legally Erroneous and an
 Abuse of Discretion

 Assignment of Error No. 2: Mr. Stewart Was Allowed to Improperly
 and Illegally Supplement the Record and His Supplement Should Be
 Stricken From the Record

 Assignment of Error No. 3: Ms. Denley Asks This Honorable Court
 For a Fair and Equitable Allocation and Assessment of Costs.
 1

 2 LAW AND DISCUSSION

 3 Peremptory Exception of No Cause of Action

 4 The recent third circuit case of Harris v. Olivier's Contractors, 14-765, pp.

 5 9-10 (La. App. 3 Cir. 12/10/14), 155 So.3d 652, 661, writ denied, 15-335 (La.

 6 4/24/015), ____So.3d____ stated:

 7 A cause of action in the context of a peremptory exception of
 8 no cause of action "refers to the operative facts which give rise to the
 9 plaintiff's right to judicially assert an action against the defendant."
10 Maw Enters., L.L.C. v. City of Marksville, 14–90, p. 6 (La.9/3/14),
11 149 So.3d 210, 215. "The purpose of the peremptory exception of no
12 cause of action is to test the legal sufficiency of the plaintiff's petition
13 by determining whether the law affords a remedy on the facts alleged
14 in the petition." Id. "The exception is triable on the face of the
15 pleadings and, for purposes of resolving the issues raised by the
16 exception, the court must presume that all well-pleaded facts in the
17 petition are true." Id.
18
19 It is the mover who has the burden of proving that a petition
20 fails to state a cause of action. Id. Review of a lower court's decision
21 on an exception of no cause of action is de novo. Id. The appellate
22 [court] must determine whether the petition states any valid cause of
23 action for relief when viewed in a light most favorable to the plaintiff
24 and with every doubt resolved in favor of the plaintiff. Id.
25
26 Generally, under LA.C.C.P. art. 931, no evidence may be
27 introduced to support or controvert the exception of no cause of
28 action." Id. at 215. "However, an exception to this rule has been
29 recognized by the jurisprudence, and a court may consider evidence
30 admitted without objection to enlarge the pleadings." Id.
31

 5
 1 Assignment of Error One

 2 The trial court in its "Judgment on Defendant in Rule's Peremptory

 3 Exception of No Cause of Action," signed on September 30, 2014, granted Mr.

 4 Stewart's peremptory exception of no cause of action, rendered the other

 5 exceptions of lack of subject matter jurisdiction and res judicata moot, and stated

 6 in pertinent part:

 7 At issue in this case is whether Ms. Denley is entitled to begin
 8 collecting her proportionate share of Mr. Stewart's retirement benefits
 9 prior to his retirement or termination from employment. Mr. Stewart
10 argues that the Apri1 27, 2010 partition judgment is a final judgment;
11 therefore, to allow Ms. Denley to begin receiving her proportionate
12 share prematurely according to the terms of that April 27, 2010
13 partition judgment amounts to a substantive amendment of a final
14 judgment. Such an amendment is an absolute nullity under Louisiana
15 law.
16
17 The Court finds Mr. Stewart's argument to have merit. The
18 Apri1 27, 2010 partition judgment was not appealed and must be
19 treated as a final judgment. According to the judgment, Ms. Denley's
20 proportionate share "shall be payable to Dianne Denley upon
21 retirement/termination of employment of James E. Stewart, Sr." Ms.
22 Denley is seeking to modify the time at which her proportionate share
23 becomes payable. An amendment generally is permissible if it "takes
24 nothing from or adds nothing to the original judgment." [Footnote
25 Three in the trial court's judgment provides: "Villaume v. Villaume,
26 363 So.2d 448 (La. 10/9/1978"] Here, Ms. Denley is requesting a
27 substantial and material modification of the April 27, 2010 judgment
28 by asking for what is due to her upon Mr. Stewart's retirement before
29 he actually retires. Without question, this is a substantive
30 modification.
31
32 While the Court has fully considered and understands Ms.
33 Denley's arguments for this Court to rely on principles of equity, the
34 Court is bound by the rule of law not to derogate from the
35 unambiguous terms of the April 27, 2010 final partition judgment.
36 Indeed, Ms. Denley's argument is certainly far short of compelling
37 when judging the exception of no cause of action from the face of the
38 pleadings. As such, the Court does not find Ms. Denley to have stated
39 a cause of action in her motion to which a remedy may be afforded,
40 and Mr. Stewart's peremptory exception of no cause of action is
41 therefore sustained.
42

 6
 1 Louisiana Code of Civil Procedure Article 1951

 2 We begin our de novo review of the trial court's ruling with La.Code Civ.P.

 3 art. 1951 which provides:

 4 On motion of the court or any party, a final judgment may be
 5 amended at any time to alter the phraseology of the judgment, but not
 6 its substance, or to correct errors of calculation. The judgment may be
 7 amended only after a hearing with notice to all parties, except that a
 8 hearing is not required if all parties consent or if the court or the party
 9 submitting the amended judgment certifies that it was provided to all
10 parties at least five days before the amendment and that no opposition
11 has been received.
12
13 The rule that a trial court may not alter the substance of a final judgment is

14 longstanding in Louisiana jurisprudence, as reiterated in Leland v. Lafayette Ins.

15 Co., 13-476, p. 6 (La.App. 3 Cir. 11/6/13), 124 So.3d 1225, 1229, writ denied, 13-

16 2814 (La. 2/14/14), 132 So.3d 967:

17 [Louisiana Code of Civil Procedure] Article 1951 contemplates the
18 correction of a "clerical error" in a final judgment, but does not
19 authorize substantive amendments. Thus, the judgment may be
20 amended by the court where the amendment takes nothing from or
21 adds nothing to the original judgment. Villaume v. Villaume, 363 So.
22 2d 448 (La. 1978). The proper recourse for an error of substance
23 within a judgment is a timely application for new trial or a timely
24 appeal. LaBove v. Theriot, 597 So.2d 1007, 1010 (La.1992); Hebert v.
25 Hebert, 351 So.2d 1199 (La.1977); Bourgeois v. Kost, 02-2785, p. 5
26 (La.5/20/03), 846 So.2d 692, 695.
27
28 The petition seeking the amendment of the April 27, 2010 Judgment does

29 not state that Ms. Denley sought either a new trial or timely appeal after the trial

30 court signed the April 27, 2010 Judgment, requested by Ms. Denley in order to

31 correct the handwritten portion of the identical judgment signed by the trial court

32 on March 15, 2010. Thus, under La.Code Civ.P. art. 1951, the April 27, 2010

33 Judgment would be considered a final judgment, which could not be amended as

34 requested in Ms. Denley's petition.

35

 7
 1 Louisiana Revised Statutes 9:2801(B)

 2 Ms. Denley's petition urges that La.R.S. 9:2801(B) provides an exception to

 3 La.Code. Civ.P. art 1951. The application of La.R.S. 9:2801(B) would negate the

 4 final judgment status of the April 27, 2010 Judgment, making it interlocutory and

 5 subject to the amendment sought in her petition. Such an application would grant

 6 Ms. Denley a cause of action and allow the trial court to consider the requested

 7 amendment to the April 27, 2010 Judgment allowing her to immediately begin to

 8 receive her allotted share of Mr. Stewart's LASERS retirement benefits or a

 9 proportionate share of his salary.

10 Louisiana Revised Statutes 9:2801(B) states:

11 B. Those provisions of a domestic relations order or other judgment
12 which partitions retirement or other deferred work benefits between
13 former spouses shall be considered interlocutory until the domestic
14 relations order has been granted "qualified" status from the plan
15 administrator and/or until the judgment has been approved by the
16 appropriate federal or state authority as being in compliance with
17 applicable laws. Amendments to this interlocutory judgment to
18 conform to the provisions of the plan shall be made with the consent
19 of the parties or following a contradictory hearing by the court which
20 granted the interlocutory judgment. The court issuing the domestic
21 relations order or judgment shall maintain continuing jurisdiction over
22 the subject matter and the parties until final resolution.
23
24 After a determination that it had jurisdiction over both the "action and these

25 parties and is the proper Court to render this Order," the April 27, 2010 Judgment

26 stated in pertinent part (emphasis added):

27 IT IS FURTHER ORDERED that this court finds that during
28 the existence of the marriage between the parties, JAMES E.
29 STEWART, SR., . . . was a member of the Louisiana State
30 Employment Retirement System (LASERS), thereby establishing a
31 community property interest in funds, DROP, survivor's or other
32 benefits that may become payable by LASERS as a result of that
33 membership in a percentage to be calculated as follows:
34
35 One-half (1/2) of a fraction, the numerator of which is the
36 number of years of service credit earned or purchased by

 8
 1 James E. Stewart, during the existence of the aforesaid
 2 community property regime from August 1, 1981 until
 3 July 12, 1999; and the denominator of which is the total
 4 number of years of service credit James E. Stewart, Sr.
 5 has with LASERS as of the date funds, survivor's, or
 6 other benefits become payable by LASERS.
 7
 8 IT IS FURTHER ORDERED that DIANNE DENLEY, shall
 9 also receive the same percentage as set forth in the proceeding section
10 of any funds deposited in James E. Stewart, Sr.'s DROP/IBO Account
11 and shall be payable to Dianne Denley upon retirement/termination of
12 employment of James E. Stewart, Sr[.]
13
14 The April 27, 2010 Judgment then ordered that the aforementioned funds be

15 payable directly to Ms. Denley at her current address or "such other address as

16 Dianne Denley may advise LASERS in writing in accordance with [the] this

17 judgment." The trial court then also ordered (emphasis added):

18 [T]hat Deanne Denley shall provide LASERS with a certified copy of
19 this judgment within thirty (30) days after it is signed by the
20 undersigned judge; and that Dianne Denley shall inform LASERS in
21 writing of all future changes in her mailing address within thirty (30)
22 days after such change of address becomes effective.
23
24 In her petition, Ms. Denley cited as support for the interlocutory status of the

25 April 27, 2010 Judgment the case of Tate v. Tate, 08-1968 (La.App. 1 Cir.

26 3/27/2009), 9 So.3d 1010. In Tate, the first circuit affirmed the trial court and

27 found the judgment at issue remained interlocutory under La.R.S. 9:2801(B). The

28 first circuit found that the judgment had not been granted "qualified" status from

29 the LASERS plan administrator, as it had been rejected for non-compliance with

30 the LASERS requirements. See La.R.S. 9:2801(B). Thus, as provided in La.R.S.

31 9:2801(B), the judgment was subject to amendment after a contradictory hearing or

32 by consent of the parties.

33 The April 27, 2010 Judgment in this case ordered that Ms. Denley, "shall

34 provide LASERS with a certified copy of this judgment within thirty (30) days

 9
 1 after it is signed by the undersigned judge." (Emphasis added.) Her petition is

 2 silent on this issue, and there is no evidence in the record that Ms. Denley did not

 3 send a certified copy of the April 27, 2010 Judgment to LASERS. This court must

 4 presume that she followed the order of the trial court and that the April 27, 2010

 5 Judgment was accepted by LASERS and, thus, was granted "qualified" status,

 6 unlike the judgment in Tate. Accordingly, the April 27, 2010 Judgment could not

 7 be considered interlocutory pursuant to La.R.S. 9:2801(B), but would constitute a

 8 final judgment under La.Code Civ.P. art. 1951.

 9 Ms. Denley also cited the cases of Hare v. Hodgins, 586 So.2d 118 (La.1991)

10 and Halverson v. Halverson, 589 So.2d 1153, (La.App 5 Cir. 1991), writ denied,

11 600 So.2d 1153 (La. 1992). Both of these cases were decided prior to the addition

12 of La.R.S 9:2801(B) to La.R.S. 9:2801 by 2001 Acts 493 and therefore lend no

13 support to Ms. Denley's argument. Additionally, each of the cases cited involved

14 an appeal of the initial partition of the community assets, which included

15 retirement benefits. As previously stated, the April 27, 2010 Judgment which

16 partitioned Mr. Stewart's retirement benefits was not appealed. Therefore, we find

17 that the trial court correctly determined that the July 31, 2014 petition filed by Ms.

18 Denley failed to state a cause of action. Thus, Ms. Denley's assignment of error

19 number one is without merit.

20 Assignment of Error Two

21 Ms. Denley seeks to have stricken from the record a September 3, 2014

22 letter from LASERS and a May 11, 2010 letter from the TRSL, which were filed in

23 conjunction with Mr. Stewart's October 24, 2014 motion to supplement the record.

24 Ms. Denley opposed the motion and there is no order allowing the submission of

25 the letters into the record. Ms. Denley sought to have the letters stricken from the

 10
 1 record and filed a "Motion and Designation of Contents of Record," which was

 2 denied by the trial court as untimely.

 3 Louisiana Code of Civil Procedure Article 2164 states, in pertinent part,

 4 "The appellate court shall render any judgment which is just, legal, and proper

 5 upon the record on appeal." "Appellate courts are courts of record and may not

 6 review evidence that is not in the appellate record, or receive new evidence."

 7 Denoux v. Vessel Mgmt. Serv., Inc., 07-2143, p. 6 (La. 5/21/08), 983 So.2d 84, 88.

 8 There is nothing in the record to indicate that the two letters were received into

 9 evidence, despite having been filed into the record in connection with the motion

10 to supplement. "Evidence not properly and officially offered and introduced

11 cannot be considered, even if it is physically placed in the record." Denoux, 983

12 So.2d at 88.

13 The trial court's ruling granting Mr. Stewart's exception of no cause of

14 action was correctly rendered on the basis of Ms. Denley's pleadings, pursuant to

15 La.Code Civ.P. art 931, which states in pertinent part, "No evidence may be

16 introduced at any time to support or controvert the objection that the petition fails

17 to state a cause of action." However, "an exception to this rule has been

18 recognized by the jurisprudence, and a court may consider evidence admitted

19 without objection to enlarge the pleadings." Maw Enters, 149 So.3d at 215. In this

20 case, the application of the exception to La.Code Civ.P art. 931 does not apply in

21 light of the strenuous objection by Ms. Denley to the admission into evidence of

22 the two letters at issue. Therefore, for the reasons previously stated, Ms. Denley's

23 assignment of error two has no merit, as the letters at issue are not part of the

24 record on appeal having not been properly entered into evidence in the district

25 court record.

 11
 1 Assignment of Error Three

 2 Ms. Denley, in her third assignment of error, asks this court to reimburse her

 3 for costs of the appeal, district court costs, and other costs relating to the

 4 proceedings. In the alternative, she asks this court for "an equitable allocation and

 5 assessment of all costs pursuant to La.Code Civ.P. art. 2164."

 6 Louisiana Code of Civil Procedure 2164 provides:

 7 The appellate court shall render any judgment which is just, legal and
 8 proper upon the record on appeal. The court may award damages,
 9 including attorney fees, for frivolous appeal or application for writs,
10 and may tax the costs of the lower or appellate court, or any part
11 thereof, against any party to the suit, as in its judgment may be
12 considered equitable.
13
14 Considering the outcome of this appeal, this court finds no basis to grant Ms.

15 Denley's request that the costs be waived or allocated between the parties to the

16 litigation.

17 CONCLUSION

18 For the reasons assigned, we affirm the judgment of the trial court sustaining

19 James E. Stewart's peremptory exception of no cause of action, finding that the

20 "Rule to Show Cause to Amend Judgment of Partition" filed on behalf of Dianne

21 Denley Stewart failed to state a cause of action for which a remedy could be

22 afforded under the laws of the State of Louisiana. Costs of this appeal are assessed

23 to Dianne Denley Stewart.

24 AFFIRMED.

25

 12
